content.text: Cost Alerts

Azure Cost Management provides three distinct alert types: budget alerts (threshold-based, configured on a budget resource), anomaly alerts (ML-detected statistical outliers against a historical baseline), and credit alerts (balance depletion warnings for EA and MCA prepaid credits). Each type serves a different monitoring use case and is configured differently.

attrs.alt: Azure cost alert types: budget, anomaly, and credit alerts with their delivery channels

content.text: Budget Alerts

Budget alerts fire when actual or forecasted spend crosses a percentage of the budget limit. They are configured as notification objects within the budget resource and support up to five threshold/type combinations per budget. See the Budgets sub-subtopic for full budget creation details; this section covers alert-specific behavior.
Alert Property
Actual
Forecasted
Trigger basis
Charges already incurred and recorded
ML projection for end-of-period total
Latency
Cost data lag (~8–24h) + check interval (~8h)
Evaluated on each check cycle with fresh projection
Re-fires?
No — fires once per threshold per period
Yes — re-fires on each check while projected to exceed
Best use
Spend control — know you've already spent X%
Early warning — prevent crossing 100% before month-end

import requests
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
token = credential.get_token("https://management.azure.com/.default").token
headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"}

# List active budget alerts for a subscription
scope = "/subscriptions/<subscription-id>"
url = (
    f"https://management.azure.com{scope}/"
    f"providers/Microsoft.CostManagement/alerts?api-version=2023-11-01"
)
resp = requests.get(url, headers=headers)
for alert in resp.json().get("value", []):
    props = alert.get("properties", {})
    print(
        f"ID: {alert['name'][:30]:<30}  "
        f"Type: {props.get('definition', {}).get('type'):<15}  "
        f"Status: {props.get('status', {}).get('value')}"
    )
Anomaly Alerts

Cost anomaly alerts use machine learning to detect when daily spending deviates significantly from the expected baseline for that scope and service combination. The baseline is trained on 60 days of historical cost data per scope. Anomalies are reported in the Cost Management portal and can send email alerts to configured recipients.
Configuring Anomaly Alerts
Retrieving Detected Anomalies Programmatically

import requests
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
token = credential.get_token("https://management.azure.com/.default").token
headers = {
    "Authorization": f"Bearer {token}",
    "Content-Type": "application/json"
}

scope = "/subscriptions/<subscription-id>"

# List anomaly alerts detected in the past 30 days
url = (
    f"https://management.azure.com{scope}/"
    f"providers/Microsoft.CostManagement/alerts?api-version=2023-11-01"
)
resp = requests.get(url, headers=headers)
anomalies = [
    a for a in resp.json().get("value", [])
    if a.get("properties", {}).get("definition", {}).get("type") == "Budget"
    or "Anomaly" in a.get("properties", {}).get("definition", {}).get("category", "")
]

for a in anomalies:
    props = a.get("properties", {})
    details = props.get("details", {})
    print(
        f"Alert: {a.get('name', '')[:40]}  "
        f"Status: {props.get('status', {}).get('value')}  "
        f"Amount: {details.get('amount', 0):>10.2f}"
    )
Credit Alerts (EA and MCA)

Credit alerts notify stakeholders when Azure credit balances drop below a specified percentage. They are configured separately from budget alerts and are only available for EA and MCA billing accounts that carry prepaid credit balances.

import requests
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
token = credential.get_token("https://management.azure.com/.default").token
headers = {
    "Authorization": f"Bearer {token}",
    "Content-Type": "application/json"
}

billing_account_name = "<billing-account-id>"
billing_profile_name = "<billing-profile-id>"

# List existing credit alerts on a billing profile
alerts_url = (
    f"https://management.azure.com/providers/Microsoft.Billing/"
    f"billingAccounts/{billing_account_name}/"
    f"billingProfiles/{billing_profile_name}/"
    f"providers/Microsoft.CostManagement/alerts?api-version=2023-11-01"
)
resp = requests.get(alerts_url, headers=headers)
for alert in resp.json().get("value", []):
    props = alert.get("properties", {})
    defn = props.get("definition", {})
    print(
        f"Alert: {alert['name'][:40]}  "
        f"Category: {defn.get('category')}  "
        f"Status: {props.get('status', {}).get('value')}"
    )
Listing All Cost Alerts Across Scopes
The following script inventories all active cost alerts across multiple subscriptions under a management group, providing a consolidated view of alert coverage.

import requests
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential

def list_all_cost_alerts(management_group_name: str) -> list:
    credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
    token = credential.get_token("https://management.azure.com/.default").token
    headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}"}

    # Get subscriptions under the management group
    mg_scope = f"/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/{management_group_name}"
    subs_url = (
        f"https://management.azure.com{mg_scope}/subscriptions?api-version=2020-05-01"
    )
    subs = requests.get(subs_url, headers=headers).json().get("value", [])

    all_alerts = []
    for sub in subs:
        sub_id = sub["name"]
        scope = f"/subscriptions/{sub_id}"
        alerts_url = (
            f"https://management.azure.com{scope}/"
            f"providers/Microsoft.CostManagement/alerts?api-version=2023-11-01"
        )
        resp = requests.get(alerts_url, headers=headers)
        if not resp.ok:
            continue
        for alert in resp.json().get("value", []):
            props = alert.get("properties", {})
            defn  = props.get("definition", {})
            all_alerts.append({
                "subscription": sub.get("displayName"),
                "alert_name": alert.get("name"),
                "type": defn.get("type"),
                "category": defn.get("category"),
                "status": props.get("status", {}).get("value"),
            })

    return all_alerts

alerts = list_all_cost_alerts("contoso-root")
print(f"{'Subscription':<30} {'Name':<35} {'Type':<15} {'Status'}")
print("-" * 90)
for a in alerts:
    print(
        f"{a['subscription']:<30} "
        f"{a['alert_name'][:35]:<35} "
        f"{a['type'] or '':<15} "
        f"{a['status'] or ''}"
    )
Cost Alert Best Practices
Practice
Rationale
Set budget alerts at 80%, 90%, and 100% Forecasted
Graduated alerts give teams time to respond before spend actually hits the limit
Enable anomaly alerts on all production subscriptions
Budget alerts only fire at known thresholds; anomalies catch unexpected spikes that don't cross a pre-set limit
Use action groups for alerts above 90%
Email-only alerts above 90% are often too late; automation (stop VMs, post to Teams) responds faster than a human can
Keep alert email lists current
Stale email addresses silently drop alerts — audit contactEmails in all budgets quarterly
Create credit alerts at 80% and 20% remaining
80% gives time to plan renewal; 20% is an emergency trigger to contact Microsoft or adjust spend immediately
Tag all budget-managed resources consistently
Budget filters on tags only catch resources with the tag; untagged resources are invisible to tag-scoped budgets
Acknowledging and Dismissing Alerts

Active cost alerts accumulate in the Cost Management alerts list. Finance teams dismiss them after reviewing or taking action. Programmatically dismissing alerts keeps the list clean and ensures only active/unreviewed alerts require attention.
Bulk Dismiss Resolved Alerts

import requests
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential

def dismiss_all_active_alerts(scope: str):
    """
    Dismiss all alerts with status 'Active' or 'InProgress' for the given scope.
    Run after reviewing and acknowledging the alert batch.
    """
    credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
    token = credential.get_token("https://management.azure.com/.default").token
    headers = {
        "Authorization": f"Bearer {token}",
        "Content-Type": "application/json"
    }

    list_url = (
        f"https://management.azure.com{scope}/"
        f"providers/Microsoft.CostManagement/alerts?api-version=2023-11-01"
    )
    resp = requests.get(list_url, headers=headers)
    alerts = resp.json().get("value", [])

    dismiss_count = 0
    for alert in alerts:
        props = alert.get("properties", {})
        status = props.get("status", {}).get("value", "")
        if status not in ("Active", "InProgress"):
            continue

        alert_name = alert["name"]
        patch_url = (
            f"https://management.azure.com{scope}/"
            f"providers/Microsoft.CostManagement/alerts/{alert_name}"
            f"?api-version=2023-11-01"
        )
        patch_resp = requests.patch(
            patch_url,
            headers=headers,
            json={"properties": {"status": {"value": "Dismissed"}}}
        )
        print(f"Dismissed [{patch_resp.status_code}]: {alert_name}")
        dismiss_count += 1

    print(f"\nTotal dismissed: {dismiss_count}")

dismiss_all_active_alerts("/subscriptions/<subscription-id>")
Sending Cost Alerts to Microsoft Teams
Teams notifications via a Logic App webhook provide richer formatting than plain email alerts, including cost breakdown tables and direct links to Cost Analysis views.

# Logic App HTTP trigger body for a Teams Adaptive Card cost alert
# Deploy as Azure Logic App triggered by an Action Group webhook

import requests
import json
from datetime import datetime

def post_cost_alert_to_teams(
    webhook_url: str,
    alert_name: str,
    subscription_name: str,
    budget_name: str,
    threshold_pct: float,
    spent_amount: float,
    budget_amount: float,
    currency: str = "USD"
):
    pct_of_budget = (spent_amount / budget_amount * 100) if budget_amount else 0
    color = "attention" if pct_of_budget >= 100 else "warning" if pct_of_budget >= 90 else "accent"
    
    adaptive_card = {
        "type": "message",
        "attachments": [
            {
                "contentType": "application/vnd.microsoft.card.adaptive",
                "content": {
                    "$schema": "http://adaptivecards.io/schemas/adaptive-card.json",
                    "type": "AdaptiveCard",
                    "version": "1.4",
                    "body": [
                        {
                            "type": "TextBlock",
                            "text": f"Azure Cost Alert: {budget_name}",
                            "weight": "Bolder",
                            "size": "Large",
                            "color": color
                        },
                        {
                            "type": "FactSet",
                            "facts": [
                                {"title": "Subscription", "value": subscription_name},
                                {"title": "Budget",       "value": budget_name},
                                {"title": "Threshold",    "value": f"{threshold_pct}%"},
                                {"title": "Spent",        "value": f"{spent_amount:,.2f} {currency}"},
                                {"title": "Budget",       "value": f"{budget_amount:,.2f} {currency}"},
                                {"title": "Utilization",  "value": f"{pct_of_budget:.1f}%"},
                                {"title": "Triggered",    "value": datetime.utcnow().strftime("%Y-%m-%d %H:%M UTC")},
                            ]
                        }
                    ],
                    "actions": [
                        {
                            "type": "Action.OpenUrl",
                            "title": "View Cost Analysis",
                            "url": "https://portal.azure.com/#view/Microsoft_Azure_CostManagement/Menu/~/costanalysis"
                        }
                    ]
                }
            }
        ]
    }

    resp = requests.post(webhook_url, json=adaptive_card)
    print(f"Teams notification: {resp.status_code}")

post_cost_alert_to_teams(
    webhook_url="https://contoso.webhook.office.com/webhookb2/...",
    alert_name="Actual_GT_80",
    subscription_name="prod-engineering-001",
    budget_name="monthly-prod-budget",
    threshold_pct=80,
    spent_amount=4250.00,
    budget_amount=5000.00,
    currency="USD"
)
Automating Alert Coverage Across New Subscriptions

In large organizations, new subscriptions are provisioned frequently. A subscription vending pipeline should include budget and anomaly alert setup as part of the provisioning workflow — not a manual afterthought.

Include cost governance setup (budget + anomaly alert) in the same pipeline as subscription vending. Alert-free subscriptions silently accumulate unchecked spend — often discovered only on the monthly invoice.
Summary

Azure provides three cost alert types: budget alerts (threshold on a budget resource), anomaly alerts (ML-detected deviations from historical baseline), and credit alerts (balance percentage drops). Budget alerts are configured inline on the budget resource with up to five notifications; anomaly alerts are configured as scheduled action rules; credit alerts are configured on MCA billing profiles or EA enrollments. Action groups extend alerts into automation — stopping VMs, posting to Teams, or triggering runbooks — transforming passive notifications into active cost control.
Budget alerts: Actual fires once per threshold per period; Forecasted re-fires every check cycle — treat forecasted as informational
Anomaly alerts require 60 days of cost history to build a baseline — new subscriptions have no anomaly detection initially
Credit alerts are only available for EA and MCA billing accounts with prepaid credit balances
All three alert types support email delivery; only budget and anomaly alerts support action group triggers
Check the Cost Management alerts API to audit all active alerts across scopes programmatically
